preFix.title: Designing a water-efficient garden
preFix.subtitle: Plant selection, soil improvement, and layout changes that cut irrigation demand
preFix.excerpt: A typical UK domestic garden during a dry summer uses 200 to 400 litres of mains water per week for lawns and borders. Replacing thirsty plants with drought-tolerant species, adding mulch to all bare soil, and grouping plants by water need can reduce irrigation demand by 40 to 60 percent without reducing the range of plants or the garden's productivity.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS guidance on drought-tolerant gardening 2024. DEFRA Water White Paper for garden water use statistics. Horticulture Week and Garden Organic guidance on mulching and water retention. Environment Agency Garden Water Use report 2021.

content.text: The highest water demand in most UK gardens comes from lawns, annual bedding, and vegetable crops. Established shrubs and perennials in good soil need little or no supplementary irrigation in most UK summers. The largest single change is replacing the lawn (or at least most of it) with planted beds and hardstanding, which eliminates the largest discretionary water use. A 50 m2 lawn that receives 20 mm of irrigation per week in a dry summer uses 1,000 litres of water per week.

During Temporary Use Bans (hosepipe bans) declared under the Water Industry Act, using a hosepipe or sprinkler on a garden is prohibited. Watering by can using water from a butt or household supply is permitted. Drought-tolerant planting and stored rainwater are the main tools for maintaining a productive garden within hosepipe ban rules.
The four changes with the highest impact
Mulch all bare soil. A 75 mm layer of
mulch

(wood chip, bark, or compost) on all planted areas reduces soil evaporation by 30 to 50 percent and suppresses weeds that compete with plants for water. Renew the mulch layer each spring to maintain the 75 mm depth.
Replace high-demand plants with
drought-tolerant
alternatives. Lavender, alliums, salvias, and achillea all do well in dry UK summers once established. Replace annual bedding with self-seeding species such as Verbena bonariensis.
Use
hydrozoning

to group plants by water need. Place salad crops, courgettes, and other high-demand vegetables together in one bed where irrigation can be targeted. Place drought-tolerant perennials and herbs in a separate bed that needs no irrigation after establishment.

Improve soil to hold more water. Adding 100 mm of compost per year raises water retention and cuts how often you need to water. Clay soils hold water better and release it more slowly with compost added. Sandy soils hold water that would otherwise drain past the roots. Fit an
evapotranspiration
-based controller on any automated system to match watering to actual plant need.
Watering technique

Water in the early morning or late evening when evaporation rates are lowest. Watering at midday on a hot day loses 20 to 30 percent of applied water to evaporation before it reaches the root zone. Water deeply and less frequently (a deep 30 mm soak once per week is more effective than a light 5 mm daily spray) to encourage deep root growth that increases drought tolerance.
What done well looks like

After one season with full mulching and drought-tolerant replanting, the vegetable beds require watering only twice per week instead of daily during a dry August. The ornamental borders need no irrigation at all after the first year. The lawn has been partially replaced with a wildflower meadow strip that needs no watering. Total garden water consumption in a dry year is 40 to 50 percent lower than before.
What done badly looks like

New drought-tolerant plants were installed but the lawn was left at full size and watered by sprinkler. The sprinkler ran for 30 minutes per day in July and August, using 300 litres per day to maintain a green lawn, cancelling the savings from the rest of the garden. Mulching was done at 25 mm depth (insufficient) and the layer dried to dust within three weeks in hot weather, providing no evaporation benefit.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Water newly planted drought-tolerant shrubs deeply once per week for the first full growing season after planting, regardless of the species' ultimate drought tolerance. The label 'drought tolerant' applies to established plants with deep root systems, not to newly planted specimens.
attrs.cause: Drought-tolerant plants still need regular watering for the first 6 to 12 months while their roots establish
attrs.symptom: Newly planted drought-tolerant shrubs dying in the first summer

attrs.fix: Remove the slimed layer and replace with fresh wood chip at 75 to 100 mm depth. Keep the mulch away from direct contact with plant stems to prevent collar rot.
attrs.cause: The mulch layer was too thin and became anaerobic as it compacted
attrs.symptom: Wood chip mulch has turned slimy and compacted

attrs.fix: Water deeply (30 mm per session) twice per week rather than lightly every day. Install a 100 mm mulch layer over the bed. Consider using a shade cloth over salad beds in July and August to reduce transpiration by 20 to 30 percent.
attrs.cause: Shallow, frequent watering is not reaching deep enough to buffer against temperature extremes at the root zone
attrs.symptom: Salad crops bolt and fail within days in hot weather despite watering
